title: Functions
description: Why functions exist: bundling code so it can be reused by name, instead of copy-pasting the same logic everywhere.
objectives: Understand what a function is and why it matters
Recognize main() as a function we've been using all along
See the shape of a simple custom function
Preview declaration, definition, calling, parameters, and return types as upcoming subtopics

sections.text: The same idea — "add two numbers and print the result" — is written out three separate times. If that logic ever needed to change, it would need to change in every single copy.
A function is a named, reusable block of code that performs a specific task and can be run (called) from anywhere by using its name.
We've actually been using a function since the very first lesson: main() is a function — the special one C++ calls automatically to start the program.
This solves the repeated-code problem: the addition logic is written exactly once, inside add, and reused three times just by calling add(...) with different values.

sections.title: At a glance
points: int — the type of value this function returns
(int a, int b) — the inputs it needs, called parameters
{ ... } — the function body, the code that actually runs
return a + b; — sends a value back to wherever the function was called from

sections.text: This is only an overview. Each of these pieces — declaring a function separately from defining it, calling it, parameters vs arguments, and return types — gets its own dedicated topic next.

sections.title: Functions give us
points: Reuse — write the logic once, use it as many times as needed
Readability — main() becomes a short list of named steps instead of one giant block
Easier fixes — a bug only needs fixing in one place, not everywhere the logic was copied
A natural way to break a big problem into smaller, named pieces

sections.title: A recipe you reuse
sections.text: A cookbook doesn't rewrite the full "how to boil pasta" instructions inside every single recipe that needs boiled pasta — it just says "boil pasta (see page 12)" and reuses that one set of steps. A function is that reusable recipe: write the steps once, refer to them by name wherever they're needed.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Copy-pasting the same logic repeatedly instead of wrapping it in a function — makes future changes error-prone, since every copy needs updating.
Thinking main() is somehow not a function — it's a function like any other, just the one C++ calls automatically to start the program.
Trying to write everything inside main() as the program grows — functions exist precisely to break large programs into smaller, named, reusable pieces.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is a function in C++?
questions.answer: A named, reusable block of code that performs a specific task and can be executed by calling its name.

questions.question: Why are functions useful?
questions.answer: They let code be written once and reused many times, improve readability, and make a program easier to maintain since a fix only needs to happen in one place.

questions.question: Is main() a function?
questions.answer: Yes — main() is a function, the special one that C++ automatically calls to begin program execution.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: A function is a named, reusable block of code that performs a specific task and can be called from anywhere by name, instead of rewriting the same logic repeatedly. We've already been using one function throughout every lesson: main(), the special function C++ calls automatically to start a program. A custom function like int add(int a, int b) { return a + b; } has a return type, a name, parameters describing its inputs, and a body — each of which gets its own dedicated topic next.

sections.title: Trick to Remember Functions
sections.text: Function = a named recipe. Write the steps once, call it by name as many times as needed.
